title: On the classification of Heegaard splittings
speaker: David Gabai

proof_stages.label: Heegaard-splitting classification problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines Heegaard splittings of closed orientable irreducible 3-manifolds, fixes the sided/isotopy convention, explains stabilization and destabilization, and formulates the goal of listing irreducible splittings without duplication.

proof_stages.label: Classical finiteness and strong irreducibility framework
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews uniqueness and finiteness results of Waldhausen, Birman, Haken-Rubinstein, Johannson, Tao Li, and Casson-Gordon, emphasizing that in the non-Haken setting irreducible splittings are strongly irreducible and finite lists exist genus by genus.

proof_stages.label: Algorithmic hyperbolic classification theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: for a closed non-Haken hyperbolic 3-manifold one can effectively compute an upper genus bound and algorithmically remove duplicate and reducible splittings from Tao Li's finite lists.

proof_stages.label: Minimal-surface and branched-surface carriers
proof_stages.summary: Using the Pitts-Rubinstein-Ketover minimal-surface picture and curvature estimates, strongly irreducible splittings are placed into finitely many eta-negatively curved branched surfaces, with index-one curvature concentration modeled by local catenoidal regions.

proof_stages.label: Polynomial growth gives an effective genus bound
proof_stages.summary: Plante's polynomial growth theorem for measured foliations, combined with exponential area growth in hyperbolic surfaces, yields a computable upper bound on intrinsic injectivity radius and hence makes the branch-surface splitting process finite and effective.

proof_stages.label: Bounded-geometry isotopies for duplicate detection
proof_stages.summary: To decide whether two listed Heegaard surfaces are isotopic, an isotopy of Heegaard foliations is replaced by a two-parameter bounded-area sweepout, and red/green compression regions are separated using strong irreducibility to thread a bounded-geometry path.

proof_stages.label: Finite crudely-normal graph for isotopy
proof_stages.summary: The bounded-geometry path space is discretized into a finite graph whose vertices are bounded-weight crudely normal or almost normal surfaces and whose edges are elementary isotopies, so connectedness in this graph decides isotopy of listed splittings.

proof_stages.label: Finite test for reducible splittings
proof_stages.summary: If a listed splitting is reducible, a bounded-area min-max comparison with an obviously destabilized foliation gives a path in the same finite graph to an obviously reducible vertex, with two triangulation scales used to distinguish apparently incompressible paths from detectable small compressions.
